<!--
Write this for a HUMAN who did NOT write the code.
  • Be clear and concrete — NOT clever. Say what it adds, what it changes, and whether anything ships.
  • Lead with plain language; move acronyms and internals BELOW the divider. Don't make the reader decode it.
  • Define every term the first time you use it. No bare cross-references (#85, "invariant 4", a rail name) —
    spell them out ("#85 — the verifier contract").
  • Use everyday use cases anyone can picture (buying a coffee, releasing a health record), not internal examples.
  • Reach for an analogy ONLY if the idea is abstract, and then just ONE everyone knows (a prepaid card, a
    house key, a receipt). No made-up scenarios, no construction metaphors, no cleverness.
Delete this comment before you submit.
-->

## In plain terms

<!-- One or two sentences a non-specialist understands: what this is and what it does. No jargon. -->

## What you're approving

<!-- What actually changes · the worst case if it's wrong · what stays safe (e.g. "no user-facing change",
     "no real money moves yet", "nothing to undo"). -->

<!-- Copy-pasteable steps: what a reviewer runs, and what they should see. -->
